description_long: This is a traditional Japanese torii gate, typically found at the entrance to Shinto shrines. The structure consists of two robust cylindrical stone pillars supporting two horizontal crossbars, with the upper crossbar gently curving upward at both ends. The surface of the stone appears weathered and mossy, suggesting age and exposure to the elements. A thick rope, known as shimenawa, is draped between the pillars and adorned with three hanging paper or straw ornaments called shide, signifying its sacred status. At the center of the upper crossbar, a rectangular plaque with Japanese characters is mounted, further emphasizing its cultural and religious significance.
description: A weathered stone Japanese torii gate with a curved top beam, shimenawa rope, and a central plaque, signifying a sacred entrance.
description_view_0: Front view showing the full torii gate, the shimenawa rope with three ornaments, and the central plaque with Japanese characters.
description_view_1: Side view highlighting the thickness of the stone pillars and the pronounced upward curve of the top crossbar.
description_view_2: Rear view of the torii gate, showing the back of the pillars and the rope, with no visible plaque.
description_view_3: Opposite side view, again emphasizing the depth and curvature of the structure, with the rope and ornaments partially visible.
